Hi @stx 
Can you please provide your `flutter doctor -v` and `flutter run --verbose`  ogs in. file (drop in the attachment), and a [minimal complete reproducible code sample](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example)?
Thank you

@TahaTesser

There is no code sample since this isn't a code problem, but a Flutter backend issue.

I found the issue: each pubspec dependency increases the time it takes to discover the observatory port. Once you hit 30s, Flutter fails.

Here's a triggering pubspec:

```
dependencies:
  flutter:
    sdk: flutter
  flutter_localizations:
    sdk: flutter
  http: ^0.12.2
  in_app_review: ^1.0.2

```

[logs.txt](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/files/5691448/logs.txt)

Problem disappeared in iOS 14.3.

Edit: Only on the first run. Debugging connected instantly after first installing iOS 14.3 on multiple devices, but then times out again on all subsequent runs.

Changing the observatory timeout to 60 seconds has fixed debugging on all devices.

It's very strange that immediately after an upgrade to 14.3, it was instant on all devices, exactly once. Seems like some weird iOS device state causing this, but deleting the app, rebooting the phones, etc. doesn't seem to make a difference.

Hi @stx 
Pretty strange, using the same packages, in fact, add more packages try to reproduce this but no crash, the app runs even several runs

Hi @TahaTesser,

1. The issue isn't a crash, it's an observatory timeout while debugging.
2. There is no code to reproduce an observatory timeout.
3. You are using a different OS, mobile device and probably not my same computer specs.

Different host OS, mobile devices, and host hardware operate at different speeds and install and launch apps in different amounts of time. Sometimes this process completes in <30 seconds (Flutter debugging works) as required by PR https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/68756 and sometimes it doesn't (Flutter debugging doesn't work.)

The problem is below and the solution is to increase the timeout.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/44d0e52d2065dbcaa01ac29b4488c7bbd7b1e76c/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart#L430

I've noticed that switching ports can make a difference.

I disconnected and reconnected the same USB-C to Lightning cable to the same port on a MBP and rebuilt over and over with the same observatory error, but the moment I changed to a different port, debugging succeeded because the install/launch process completed much more quickly.

This is indicative of a low level Xcode or MacOS issue - throughput appears to get degraded for some reason and hit this 30s timeout.

I think it would be valuable if you're able to reproduce this issue to open **System Report > Hardware > USB > [Device Name]** and report the **Speed** flag, e.g. **Up to 480 Mb/s**. Perhaps the degradation is reported here, but maybe not.

Either way, I would strongly recommend trying other ports.

For those of you that want to use the workaround of changing the observatory timeout to 60 seconds, you'll need to force snapshot regeneration for `flutter_tools` by running this command `rm /{$PATH_TO_FLUTTER_DIRECTORY}/bin/cache/flutter_tools.stamp /{$PATH_TO_FLUTTER_DIRECTORY}/bin/cache/flutter_tools.snapshot` as mentioned here:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/tree/master/packages/flutter_tools#forcing-snapshot-regeneration

You'll need to do this because even if you change the observatory timeout in the dart code, the old `flutter_tools` snapshot will still be used unless you for it to regenerate.

In my case this was due to the device cache not being able to clear.

Xcode -> Window -> Devices & Simulators. Select the problem device (an iPad in my instance).

A yellow banner was displayed above showing the device stuck trying to clear the cache. 

I removed all installed apps from this screen by hitting selecting all and using the "-" button and the cache issue resolved. Am now able to install with debug mode as normal.

I sloved this problem:
1. set the observatory timeout to 90 seconds, flutter/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart
`localUri = await observatoryDiscovery.uri.timeout(const Duration(seconds: 90)); `
2. remove flutter_tools.stamp and 
`rm flutter/bin/cache/flutter_tools.stamp
rm flutter/bin/cache/flutter_tools.snapshot`

I tried every solutions above but still failed to fix it. However after cleaning `Derived Data` in Xcode, it magically worked...
In `XCode -> Preference -> Locations -> Derived Data`, click the little arrow to open the path in finder and clean the folder. Then `flutter run` resume working normally.
